answer: at the beginning of a word
reasoning: Looking at words like yrama, ytonoroipory, ytuwaro - when y appears at the beginning, it's silent in the phonetic transcription
In kynapojaton, the y after n is pronounced as /j/
When y is pronounced after n, it becomes the phoneme /j/
In yjenarikepy, y appears before j and is pronounced as /j/
When y appears before j, it's pronounced as /j/

answer: at the end of a word
reasoning: In words ending with y like anipynary, uwēposapariky, the y is silent
Y can indicate palatalization of the preceding consonant
Otherwise, y is pronounced as /i/

answer: at the beginning of a word
reasoning: p, t, k remain voiceless at the beginning of words
p, t, k become voiced between vowels
p, t, k become their voiced counterparts b, d, g between vowels
After nasals, these consonants also become voiced
After nasals, they become voiced
Otherwise they remain voiceless
Following the rules: k between vowels stays k, p between vowels stays p, y at end is silent
t between vowels stays t, p between vowels stays p
k at beginning stays k, k between vowels stays k
p at beginning stays p, y at end is silent becoming i
k at beginning stays k, j represents the j sound
y at beginning is silent or becomes i, k between vowels stays k, y at end becomes i
t at beginning stays t, y after t indicates palatalization or becomes i, k and p between vowels stay unchanged, y at end becomes i
